11.6.      Variance Reduction Techniques

Let X1 , . . . , Xn have a given joint distribution, and suppose we are interested in
computing

                                Œ∏ ‚â° E[g(X1 , . . . , Xn )]

where g is some specified function. It is often the case that it is not possible to
analytically compute the preceding, and when such is the case we can attempt
                                                                      (1)         (1)
to use simulation to estimate Œ∏ . This is done as follows: Generate X1 , . . . , Xn
having the same joint distribution as X1 , . . . , Xn and set
                                      (1)              
                             Y1 = g X1 , . . . , Xn(1)

Now, simulate a second set of random variables (independent of the first set)
 (2)          (2)
X1 , . . . , Xn having the distribution of X1 , . . . , Xn and set
                                      (2)               
                               Y2 = g X1 , . . . , Xn(2)

Continue this until you have generated k (some predetermined number) sets,
and so have also computed Y1 , Y2 , . . . , Yk . Now, Y1 , . . . , Yk are independent and
identically distributed random variables each having the same distribution of
g(X1 , . . . , Xn ). Thus, if we let YÃÑ denote the average of these k random variables‚Äî
that is,
                                             
                                             k
                                      YÃÑ =         Yi /k
                                             i=1

then

                                      E[YÃÑ ] = Œ∏,
                                "          #
                               E (YÃÑ ‚àí Œ∏ )2 = Var(YÃÑ )
704     11   Simulation


Hence, we can use YÃÑ as an estimate of Œ∏ . As the expected square of the difference
between YÃÑ and Œ∏ is equal to the variance of YÃÑ , we would like this quantity to be
as small as possible. [In the preceding situation, Var(YÃÑ ) = Var(Yi )/k, which is
usually not known in advance but must be estimated from the generated values
Y1 , . . . , Yn .] We now present three general techniques for reducing the variance of
our estimator.

11.6.1. Use of Antithetic Variables
In the preceding situation, suppose that we have generated Y1 and Y2 , identically
distributed random variables having mean Œ∏ . Now,
                         
                  Y1 + Y2      1
             Var             = [Var(Y1 ) + Var(Y2 ) + 2 Cov(Y1 , Y2 )]
                     2         4
                                    Var(Y1 ) Cov(Y1 , Y2 )
                                =           +
                                       2         2
Hence, it would be advantageous (in the sense that the variance would be reduced)
if Y1 and Y2 rather than being independent were negatively correlated. To see
how we could arrange this, let us suppose that the random variables X1 , . . . , Xn
are independent and, in addition, that each is simulated via the inverse transform
technique. That is, Xi is simulated from Fi‚àí1 (Ui ) where Ui is a random number
and Fi is the distribution of Xi . Hence, Y1 can be expressed as
                                                             
                         Y1 = g F1‚àí1 (U1 ), . . . , Fn‚àí1 (Un )

Now, since 1 ‚àí U is also uniform over (0, 1) whenever U is a random number
(and is negatively correlated with U ) it follows that Y2 defined by
                                                                
                    Y2 = g F1‚àí1 (1 ‚àí U1 ), . . . , Fn‚àí1 (1 ‚àí Un )

will have the same distribution as Y1 . Hence, if Y1 and Y2 were negatively corre-
lated, then generating Y2 by this means would lead to a smaller variance than if it
were generated by a new set of random numbers. (In addition, there is a computa-
tional savings since rather than having to generate n additional random numbers,
we need only subtract each of the previous n from 1.) The following theorem
will be the key to showing that this technique‚Äîknown as the use of antithetic
variables‚Äîwill lead to a reduction in variance whenever g is a monotone function.
Theorem 11.1 If X1 , . . . , Xn are independent, then, for any increasing func-
tions f and g of n variables,

                          E[f (X)g(X)] ‚©æ E[f (X)]E[g(X)]                       (11.11)

where X = (X1 , . . . , Xn ).
                                              11.6.   Variance Reduction Techniques    705

Proof The proof is by induction on n. To prove it when n = 1, let f and g be
increasing functions of a single variable. Then, for any x and y,

                             (f (x) ‚àí f (y))(g(x) ‚àí g(y)) ‚©æ 0

since if x ‚©æ y (x ‚©Ω y) then both factors are nonnegative (nonpositive). Hence, for
any random variables X and Y ,

                            (f (X) ‚àí f (Y ))(g(X) ‚àí g(Y )) ‚©æ 0

implying that
                          E[(f (X) ‚àí f (Y ))(g(X) ‚àí g(Y ))] ‚©æ 0
or, equivalently,

         E[f (X)g(X)] + E[f (Y )g(Y )] ‚©æ E[f (X)g(Y )] + E[f (Y )g(X)]

If we suppose that X and Y are independent and identically distributed then, as in
this case,

                 E[f (X)g(X)] = E[f (Y )g(Y )],
                 E[f (X)g(Y )] = E[f (Y )g(X)] = E[f (X)]E[g(X)]

we obtain the result when n = 1.
  So assume that (11.11) holds for n ‚àí 1 variables, and now suppose that
X1 , . . . , Xn are independent and f and g are increasing functions. Then

    E[f (X)g(X)|Xn = xn ]
         = E[f (X1 , . . . , Xn‚àí1 , xn )g(X1 , . . . , Xn‚àí1 , xn )|Xn = x]
         = E[f (X1 , . . . , Xn‚àí1 , xn )g(X1 , . . . , Xn‚àí1 , xn )]      by independence
         ‚©æ E[f (X1 , . . . , Xn‚àí1 , xn )]E[g(X1 , . . . , Xn‚àí1 , xn )]
              by the induction hypothesis
         = E[f (X)|Xn = xn ]E[g(X)|Xn = xn ]

Hence,
                     E[f (X)g(X)|Xn ] ‚©æ E[f (X)|Xn ]E[g(X)|Xn ]
and, upon taking expectations of both sides,
                                    "                    #
                 E[f (X)g(X)] ‚©æ E E[f (X)|Xn ]E[g(X)|Xn ]
                                       ‚©æ E[f (X)]E[g(X)]
706     11     Simulation


The last inequality follows because E[f (X)|Xn ] and E[g(X)|Xn ] are both in-
creasing functions of Xn , and so, by the result for n = 1,
         "                       #   "            # "           #
        E E[f (X)|Xn ]E[g(X)|Xn ] ‚©æ E E[f (X)|Xn ] E E[g(X)|Xn ]
                                              = E[f (X)]E[g(X)] 

Corollary 11.7 If U1 , . . . , Un are independent, and k is either an increasing
or decreasing function, then
                                                                       
                    Cov k(U1 , . . . , Un ), k(1 ‚àí U1 , . . . , 1 ‚àí Un ) ‚©Ω 0

Proof Suppose k is increasing. As ‚àík(1 ‚àí U1 , . . . , 1 ‚àí Un ) is increasing in
U1 , . . . , Un , then, from Theorem 11.1,
                                                                       
                   Cov k(U1 , . . . , Un ), ‚àík(1 ‚àí U1 , . . . , 1 ‚àí Un ) ‚©æ 0

When k is decreasing just replace k by its negative.             
   Since Fi‚àí1 (Ui ) is increasing in Ui (as Fi , being a distribution function, is in-
creasing) it follows that g(F1‚àí1 (U1 ), . . . , Fn‚àí1 (Un )) is a monotone function of
U1 , . . . , Un whenever g is monotone. Hence, if g is monotone the antithetic vari-
able approach of twice using each set of random numbers U1 , . . . , Un by first com-
puting g(F1‚àí1 (U1 ), . . . , Fn‚àí1 (Un )) and then g(F1‚àí1 (1 ‚àí U1 ), . . . , Fn‚àí1 (1 ‚àí Un ))
will reduce the variance of the estimate of E[g(X1 , . . . , Xn )]. That is, rather than
generating k sets of n random numbers, we should generate k/2 sets and use each
set twice.
Example 11.14 (Simulating the Reliability Function) Consider a system of
n components in which component i, independently of other components, works
with probability pi , i = 1, . . . , n. Letting
                               
                                  1,       if component i works
                        Xi =
                                  0,       otherwise

suppose there is a monotone structure function œÜ such that
                             
                              1,  if the system works under X1 , . . . , Xn
       œÜ(X1 , . . . , Xn ) =
                              0,  otherwise

We are interested in using simulation to estimate

             r(p1 , . . . , pn ) ‚â° E[œÜ(X1 , . . . , Xn )] = P {œÜ(X1 , . . . , Xn ) = 1}
                                              11.6.   Variance Reduction Techniques      707

Now, we can simulate the Xi by generating uniform random numbers U1 , . . . , Un
and then setting
                                
                                 1,      if Ui < pi
                          Xi =
                                 0,      otherwise

Hence, we see that

                            œÜ(X1 , . . . , Xn ) = k(U1 , . . . , Un )

where k is a decreasing function of U1 , . . . , Un . Hence,

                                 Cov(k(U), k(1 ‚àí U)) ‚©Ω 0

and so the antithetic variable approach of using U1 , . . . , Un to generate both
k(U1 , . . . , Un ) and k(1 ‚àí U1 , . . . , 1 ‚àí Un ) results in a smaller variance than if
an independent set of random numbers was used to generate the second k. 
Example 11.15 (Simulating a Queueing System) Consider a given queueing
system, and let Di denote the delay in queue of the ith arriving customer, and
suppose we are interested in simulating the system so as to estimate

                                  Œ∏ = E[D1 + ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ + Dn ]

Let X1 , . . . , Xn denote the first n interarrival times and S1 , . . . , Sn the first n ser-

dent. Now in most systems D1 + ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ + Dn will be a function of X1 , . . . , Xn ,
S1 , . . . , Sn ‚Äîsay,

                     D1 + ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ + Dn = g(X1 , . . . , Xn , S1 , . . . , Sn )

Also g will usually be increasing in Si and decreasing in Xi , i = 1, . . . , n.

dent uniform random numbers‚Äîthen we may write

where k is increasing in its variates. Hence, the antithetic variable approach will

1, . . . , n and set Xi = Fi‚àí1 (1 ‚àí Ui ) and Yi = G‚àí1   i (UÃÑi ) for the first run, and

11.6.2. Variance Reduction by Conditioning

Let us start by recalling (see Proposition 3.1) the conditional variance formula

                      Var(Y ) = E[Var(Y |Z)] + Var(E[Y |Z])                     (11.12)

Now suppose we are interested in estimating E[g(X1 , . . . , Xn )] by simulating
X = (X1 , . . . , Xn ) and then computing Y = g(X1 , . . . , Xn ). Now, if for some ran-
dom variable Z we can compute E[Y |Z] then, as Var(Y |Z) ‚©æ 0, it follows from
the conditional variance formula that

                               Var(E[Y |Z]) ‚©Ω Var(Y )

implying, since E[E[Y |Z]] = E[Y ], that E[Y |Z] is a better estimator of E[Y ]
than is Y .
  In many situations, there are a variety of Zi that can be conditioned on to obtain
an improved estimator. Each of these estimators E[Y |Zi ] will have mean E[Y ]
and smaller variance than does the raw estimator
                                                     Y . We now show that for any
choice of weights Œªi , Œªi ‚©æ 0, i Œªi = 1, i Œªi E[Y |Zi ] is also an improvement
over Y .
                                         
Proposition 11.8 For any Œªi ‚©æ 0, ‚àû          i=1 Œªi = 1,
           
   (a) E[ i Œªi E[Y |Zi ]] = E[Y ],
   (b) Var( i Œªi E[Y |Zi ]) ‚©Ω Var(Y ).

Proof The proof of (a) is immediate. To prove (b), let N denote an integer
valued random variable independent of all the other random variables under con-
sideration and such that

                             P {N = i} = Œªi ,       i‚©æ1

Applying the conditional variance formula twice yields

                      Var(Y ) ‚©æ Var(E[Y |N, ZN ])
                                                             
                              ‚©æ Var E[E[Y |N, ZN ]|Z1 , . . .]
                                    
                              = Var   Œªi E[Y |Zi ] 
                                       i

Example 11.16 Consider a queueing system having Poisson arrivals and
suppose that any customer arriving when there are already N others in the system
is lost. Suppose that we are interested in using simulation to estimate the expected
                                             11.6.     Variance Reduction Techniques      709

number of lost customers by time t. The raw simulation approach would be to
simulate the system up to time t and determine L, the number of lost customers
for that run. A better estimate, however, can be obtained by conditioning on the
total time in [0, t] that the system is at capacity. Indeed, if we let T denote the
time in [0, t] that there are N in the system, then

                                        E[L|T ] = ŒªT

where Œª is the Poisson arrival rate. Hence, a better estimate for E[L] than the
average value of L over all simulation runs can be obtained by multiplying the
average value of T per simulation run by Œª. If the arrival process were a nonho-
mogeneous Poisson process, then we could improve over the raw estimator L by
keeping track of those time periods for which the system is at capacity. If we let
I1 , . . . , IC denote the time intervals in [0, t] in which there are N in the system,
then

                                                      
                                                      C
                            E[L|I1 , . . . , IC ] =            Œª(s) ds
                                                      i=1 Ii

where Œª(s) is the intensity function of the nonhomogeneous Poisson arrival
process. The use of the right side of the preceding would thus lead to a better
estimate of E[L] than the raw estimator L. 

mediately apparent that ni=1 E[Wi |Yi ] has smaller variance than ni=1 Wi .) For

instance, in the model G/M/1

Example 11.18 (Estimating the Renewal Function by Simulation) Consider
a queueing model in which customers arrive daily in accordance with a renewal
process having interarrival distribution F . However, suppose that at some fixed
time T , for instance 5 P.M., no additional arrivals are permitted and those cus-
tomers that are still in the system are serviced. At the start of the next, and each
succeeding, day customers again begin to arrive in accordance with the renewal
process. Suppose we are interested in determining the average time that a cus-
tomer spends in the system. Upon using the theory of renewal reward processes
(with a cycle starting every T time units), it can be shown that

             average time that a customer spends in the system
                   E[sum of the times in the system of arrivals in (0, T )]
               =
                                           m(T )

where m(T ) is the expected number of renewals in (0, T ).
   If we were to use simulation to estimate the preceding quantity, a run would
consist of simulating a single day, and as part of a simulation run, we would
observe the quantity N (T ), the number of arrivals by time T . Since E[N (T )] =
m(T ), the natural simulation estimator of m(T ) would be the average (over all
simulated days) value of N (T ) obtained. However, Var(N (T )) is, for large T ,
proportional to T (its asymptotic form being T œÉ 2 /Œº3 , where œÉ 2 is the variance
and Œº the mean of the interarrival distribution F ), and so, for large T , the variance
of our estimator would be large. A considerable improvement can be obtained by
using the analytic formula (see Section 7.3)

                                       T     E[Y (T )]
                             m(T ) =     ‚àí1+                                   (11.13)
                                       Œº        Œº

where Y (T ) denotes the time from T until the next renewal‚Äîthat is, it is the
excess life at T . Since the variance of Y (T ) does not grow with T (indeed, it
converges to a finite value provided the moments of F are finite), it follows that
for T large, we would do much better by using the simulation to estimate E[Y (T )]
and then use Equation (11.13) to estimate m(T ).
                                       11.6.   Variance Reduction Techniques    711




                              Figure 11.5. A(T ) = x.


   However, by employing conditioning, we can improve further on our estimate
of m(T ). To do so, let A(T ) denote the age of the renewal process at time T ‚Äîthat
is, it is the time at T since the last renewal. Then, rather than using the value of
Y (T ), we can reduce the variance by considering E[Y (T )|A(T )]. Now knowing
that the age at T is equal to x is equivalent to knowing that there was a renewal at
time T ‚àí x and the next interarrival time X is greater than x. Since the excess at
T will equal X ‚àí x (see Figure 11.5), it follows that

                  E[Y (T )|A(T ) = x] = E[X ‚àí x|X > x]
                                         ‚àû
                                            P {X ‚àí x > t}
                                      =                    dt
                                         0    P {X > x}
                                         ‚àû
                                            [1 ‚àí F (t + x)]
                                      =                     dt
                                         0     1 ‚àí F (x)

which can be numerically evaluated if necessary.
  As an illustration of the preceding note that if the renewal process is a Poisson
process with rate Œª, then the raw simulation estimator N (T ) will have variance
ŒªT ; since Y (T ) will be exponential with rate Œª, the estimator based on (11.13)
will have variance Œª2 Var{Y (T )} = 1. On the other hand, since Y (T ) will be inde-
pendent of A(T ) (and E[Y (T )|A(T )] = 1/Œª), it follows that the variance of the
improved estimator E[Y (T )|A(T )] is 0. That is, conditioning on the age at time
T yields, in this case, the exact answer. 
Example 11.19 Consider the M/G/1 queueing system where customers ar-
rive in accordance with a Poisson process with rate Œª to a single server having
service distribution G with mean E[S]. Suppose that, for a specified time t0 , the
server will take a break at the first time t ‚©æ t0 at which the system is empty. That
is, if X(t) is the number of customers in the system at time t, then the server will
take a break at time

                            T = min{t ‚©æ t0 : X(t) = 0}

To efficiently use simulation to estimate E[T ], generate the system to time t0 ; let
R denote the remaining service time of the customer in service at time t0 , and
let XQ equal the number of customers waiting in queue at time t0 . (Note that R
is equal to 0 if X(t0 ) = 0, and XQ = (X(t0 ) ‚àí 1)+ .) Now, with N equal to the
number of customers that arrive in the remaining service time R, it follows that
712     11    Simulation


if N = n and XQ = nQ , then the additional amount of time from t0 + R until the
server can take a break is equal to the amount of time that it takes until the system,
starting with n + nQ customers, becomes empty. Because this is equal to the sum
of n + nQ busy periods, it follows from Section 8.5.3 that

                                                             E[S]
                  E[T |R, N, XQ ] = t0 + R + (N + XQ )
                                                          1 ‚àí ŒªE[S]
Consequently,
                              "                      #
              E[T |R, XQ ] = E E[T |R, N, XQ ]|R, XQ
                                                                 E[S]
                            = t0 + R + (E[N |R, XQ ] + XQ )
                                                              1 ‚àí ŒªE[S]
                                                       E[S]
                            = t0 + R + (ŒªR + XQ )
                                                    1 ‚àí ŒªE[S]
Thus, rather than using the generated value of T as the estimator from a sim-
ulation run, it is better to stop the simulation at time t0 and use the estimator
                   E[S]
t0 + (ŒªR + XQ ) 1‚àíŒªE[S]   . 

11.6.3. Control Variates
Again suppose we want to use simulation to estimate E[g(X)] where X =
(X1 , . . . , Xn ). But now suppose that for some function f the expected value of
f (X) is known‚Äîsay, E[f (X)] = Œº. Then for any constant a we can also use

                              W = g(X) + a(f (X) ‚àí Œº)

as an estimator of E[g(X)]. Now,

             Var(W ) = Var(g(X)) + a 2 Var(f (X)) + 2a Cov(g(X), f (X))

Simple calculus shows that the preceding is minimized when
                                   ‚àí Cov(f (X), g(X))
                              a=
                                       Var(f (X))
and, for this value of a,

                                            [Cov(f (X), g(X))]2
                    Var(W ) = Var(g(X)) ‚àí
                                                Var(f (X))
Because Var(f (X)) and Cov(f (X), g(X)) are usually unknown, the simulated
data should be used to estimate these quantities.
                                         11.6.    Variance Reduction Techniques   713

  Dividing the preceding equation by Var(g(X)) shows that

                        Var(W )
                                 = 1 ‚àí Corr2 (f (X), g(X))
                       Var(g(X))

where Corr(X, Y ) is the correlation between X and Y . Consequently, the use of a
control variate will greatly reduce the variance of the simulation estimator when-
ever f (X) and g(X) are strongly correlated.

Example 11.20 Consider a continuous time Markov chain which, upon en-
tering state i, spends an exponential time with rate vi in that state before making
a transition into some other state, with the transition being into state j with prob-
ability Pi,j , i ‚©æ 0, j = i. Suppose that costs are incurred at rate C(i) ‚©æ 0 per unit
time whenever the chain is in state i, i ‚©æ 0. With X(t) equal to the state at time t,
and Œ± being a constant such that 0 < Œ± < 1, the quantity

                                    ‚àû
                             W=          e‚àíŒ±t C(X(t)) dt
                                    0


represents the total discounted cost. For a given initial state, suppose we want
to use simulation to estimate E[W ]. Whereas at first it might seem that we can-
not obtain an unbiased estimator without simulating the continuous time Markov
chain for an infinite amount of time (which is clearly impossible), we can make
use of the results of Example 5.1 which gives the equivalent expression for E[W ]:

                                        . T                  /
                           E[W ] = E             C(X(t)) dt
                                          0


where T is an exponential random variable with rate Œ± that is independent of the
continuous time Markov chain. Therefore, we can first generate the value of T ,
then generate the states of the continuous time Markov chain up to time T , to
                                 T
obtain the unbiased estimator 0 C(X(t)) dt. Because all the costs rates are non-
negative this estimator is strongly positively correlated with T , which will thus
make an effective control variate. 

11.6.4. Importance Sampling
Let X = (X1 , . . . , Xn ) denote a vector of random variables having a joint density
function (or joint mass function in the discrete case) f (x) = f (x1 , . . . , xn ), and
suppose that we are interested in estimating
                                             
                             Œ∏ = E[h(X)] = h(x)f (x) dx

where the preceding is an n-dimensional integral. (If the Xi are discrete, then
interpret the integral as an n-fold summation.)
                                         11.6.   Variance Reduction Techniques     715

   Suppose that a direct simulation of the random vector X, so as to compute val-
ues of h(X), is inefficient, possibly because (a) it is difficult to simulate a random
vector having density function f (x), or (b) the variance of h(X) is large, or (c) a
combination of (a) and (b).
   Another way in which we can use simulation to estimate Œ∏ is to note that if g(x)
is another probability density such that f (x) = 0 whenever g(x) = 0, then we can
express Œ∏ as
                                     
                                      h(x)f (x)
                                Œ∏=              g(x) dx
                                         g(x)
                                      .           /
                                        h(X)f (X)
                                 = Eg                                            (11.14)
                                          g(X)

where we have written Eg to emphasize that the random vector X has joint den-
sity g(x).
   It follows from Equation (11.14) that Œ∏ can be estimated by successively gen-
erating values of a random vector X having density function g(x) and then using
as the estimator the average of the values of h(X)f (X)/g(X). If a density func-
tion g(x) can be chosen so that the random variable h(X)f (X)/g(X) has a small
variance then this approach‚Äîreferred to as importance sampling‚Äîcan result in
an efficient estimator of Œ∏ .
   Let us now try to obtain a feel for why importance sampling can be useful. To
begin, note that f (X) and g(X) represent the respective likelihoods of obtaining
the vector X when X is a random vector with respective densities f and g. Hence,
if X is distributed according to g, then it will usually be the case that f (X) will
be small in relation to g(X) and thus when X is simulated according to g the
likelihood ratio f (X)/g(X) will usually be small in comparison to 1. However, it
is easy to check that its mean is 1:
                      .         /                 
                          f (X)     f (x)
                 Eg              =        g(x) dx = f (x) dx = 1
                          g(X)      g(x)

Thus we see that even though f (X)/g(X) is usually smaller than 1, its mean is
equal to 1; thus implying that it is occasionally large and so will tend to have a
large variance. So how can h(X)f (X)/g(X) have a small variance? The answer is
that we can sometimes arrange to choose a density g such that those values of x for
which f (x)/g(x) is large are precisely the values for which h(x) is exceedingly
small, and thus the ratio h(X)f (X)/g(X) is always small. Since this will require
that h(x) sometimes be small, importance sampling seems to work best when
estimating a small probability; for in this case the function h(x) is equal to 1
when x lies in some set and is equal to 0 otherwise.
716    11   Simulation


   We will now consider how to select an appropriate density g. We
                                                                  will find that
the so-called tilted densities are useful. Let M(t) = Ef [etX ] = etx f (x) dx be
the moment generating function corresponding to a one-dimensional density f .

Definition 11.2 A density function

                                              etx f (x)
                                   ft (x) =
                                               M(t)

is called a tilted density of f , ‚àí‚àû < t < ‚àû.

A random variable with density ft tends to be larger than one with density f when
t > 0 and tends to be smaller when t < 0.
   In certain cases the tilted distributions ft have the same parametric form as
does f .

Example 11.22 If f is the exponential density with rate Œª then

                          ft (x) = Cetx Œªe‚àíŒªx = ŒªCe‚àí(Œª‚àít)x

where C = 1/M(t) does not depend on x. Therefore, for t ‚©Ω Œª, ft is an exponen-
tial density with rate Œª ‚àí t.
   If f is a Bernoulli probability mass function with parameter p, then

                         f (x) = p x (1 ‚àí p)1‚àíx ,         x = 0, 1

Hence, M(t) = Ef [etX ] = pet + 1 ‚àí p and so

                              1
                 ft (x) =         (pet )x (1 ‚àí p)1‚àíx
                            M(t)
                                           x              1‚àíx
                                   pet              1‚àíp
                          =
                              pet + 1 ‚àí p        pet + 1 ‚àí p

That is, ft is the probability mass function of a Bernoulli random variable with
parameter

                                             pet
                                  pt =
                                         pet + 1 ‚àí p

We leave it as an exercise to show that if f is a normal density with parameters Œº
and œÉ 2 then ft is a normal density mean Œº + œÉ 2 t and variance œÉ 2 . 
                                             11.6.    Variance Reduction Techniques   717

  In certain situations the quantity of interest is the sum of the independent ran-
dom variables X1 , . . . , Xn . In this case the joint density f is the product of one-
dimensional densities. That is,

                          f (x1 , . . . , xn ) = f1 (x1 ) ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ fn (xn )

where fi is the density function of Xi . In this situation it is often useful to generate
the Xi according to their tilted densities, with a common choice of t employed.
Example 11.23 Let X1 , . . . , Xn be independent random variables having re-
spective probability density (or mass) functions fi , for i = 1, . . . , n. Suppose we

a, where a is much larger than the mean of the sum. That is, we are interested in

                                  Œ∏ = P {S ‚©æ a}

where f = (f1 , . . . , fn ). Suppose now that we simulate Xi according to the tilted

Example 11.24 Consider a single-server queue in which the times between
successive customer arrivals have density function f and the service times have
density g. Let Dn denote the amount of time that the nth arrival spends wait-
ing in queue and suppose we are interested in estimating Œ± = P {Dn ‚©æ a} when
a is much larger than E[Dn ]. Rather than generating the successive interarrival
and service times according to f and g, respectively, they should be generated
according to the densities f‚àít and gt , where t is a positive number to be deter-
mined. Note that using these distributions as opposed to f and g will result in
smaller interarrival times (since ‚àít < 0) and larger service times. Hence, there
will be a greater chance that Dn > a than if we had simulated using the densities
f and g. The importance sampling estimator of Œ± would then be

                   Œ±ÃÇ = I {Dn > a}et (Sn ‚àíYn ) [Mf (‚àít)Mg (t)]n

where Sn is the sum of the first n interarrival times, Yn is the sum of the first
n service times, and Mf and Mg are the moment generating functions of the
densities f and g, respectively. The value of t used should be determined by
experimenting with a variety of different choices. 
720     11   Simulation


11.7.        Determining the Number of Runs

Suppose that we are going to use simulation to generate r independent and identi-
cally distributed random variables Y (1) , . . . , Y (r) having mean Œº and variance œÉ 2 .
We are then going to use

                                       Y (1) + ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ + Y (r)
                               YÃÑr =
                                                r
as an estimate of Œº. The precision of this estimate can be measured by its variance

                               Var(YÃÑr ) = E[(YÃÑr ‚àí Œº)2 ]
                                         = œÉ 2 /r

Hence we would want to choose r, the number of necessary runs, large enough so
that œÉ 2 /r is acceptably small. However, the difficulty is that œÉ 2 is not known in
advance. To get around this, you should initially simulate k runs (where k ‚©æ 30)
and then use the simulated values Y (1) , . . . , Y (k) to estimate œÉ 2 by the sample
variance
                               
                               k
                                  (i)   2
                                  Y ‚àí YÃÑk /(k ‚àí 1)
                               i=1

Based on this estimate of œÉ 2 the value of r that attains the desired level of preci-
sion can now be determined and an additional r ‚àí k runs can be generated.



11.8.        Coupling from the Past

Consider an irreducible Markov chain with states 1, . . . , m and transition proba-
bilities Pi,j and suppose we want to generate the value of a random variable whose
distribution is that of the stationary distribution of this Markov chain. Whereas we
could approximately generate such a random variable by arbitrarily choosing an
initial state, simulating the resulting Markov chain for a large fixed number of
time periods, and then choosing the final state as the value of the random vari-
able, we will now present a procedure that generates a random variable whose
distribution is exactly that of the stationary distribution.
   If, in theory, we generated the Markov chain starting at time ‚àí‚àû in any ar-
bitrary state, then the state at time 0 would have the stationary distribution. So
imagine that we do this, and suppose that a different person is to generate the
next state at each of these times. Thus, if X(‚àín), the state at time ‚àín, is i, then
                                                    11.8.      Coupling from the Past   721

person ‚àín would generate a random variable that is equal to j with probability
Pi,j , j = 1, . . . , m, and the value generated would be the state at time ‚àí(n ‚àí 1).
Now suppose that person ‚àí1 wants to do his random variable generation early.
Because he does not know what the state at time ‚àí1 will be, he generates a se-
quence of random variables N‚àí1 (i), i = 1, . . . , m, where N‚àí1 (i), the next state
if X(‚àí1) = i, is equal to j with probability Pi,j , j = 1, . . . , m. If it results that
X(‚àí1) = i, then person ‚àí1 would report that the state at time 0 is

                         S‚àí1 (i) = N‚àí1 (i),             i = 1, . . . , m

(That is, S‚àí1 (i) is the simulated state at time 0 when the simulated state at time
‚àí1 is i.)
   Now suppose that person ‚àí2, hearing that person ‚àí1 is doing his simula-
tion early, decides to do the same thing. She generates a sequence of random
variables N‚àí2 (i), i = 1, . . . , m, where N‚àí2 (i) is equal to j with probability
Pi,j , j = 1, . . . , m. Consequently, if it is reported to her that X(‚àí2) = i, then
she will report that X(‚àí1) = N‚àí2 (i). Combining this with the early generation of
person ‚àí1 shows that if X(‚àí2) = i, then the simulated state at time 0 is

                     S‚àí2 (i) = S‚àí1 (N‚àí2 (i)),              i = 1, . . . , m

  Continuing in the preceding manner, suppose that person ‚àí3 generates a se-
quence of random variables N‚àí3 (i), i = 1, . . . , m, where N‚àí3 (i) is to be the gen-
erated value of the next state when X(‚àí3) = i. Consequently, if X(‚àí3) = i then
the simulated state at time 0 would be

                     S‚àí3 (i) = S‚àí2 (N‚àí3 (i)),              i = 1, . . . , m

  Now suppose we continue the preceding, and so obtain the simulated
functions

                   S‚àí1 (i), S‚àí2 (i), S‚àí3 (i), . . . ,         i = 1, . . . , m

Going backward in time in this manner, we will at some time, say ‚àír, have a
simulated function S‚àír (i) that is a constant function. That is, for some state j ,
S‚àír (i) will equal j for all states i = 1, . . . , m. But this means that no matter what
the simulated values from time ‚àí‚àû to ‚àír, we can be certain that the simulated
value at time 0 is j . Consequently, j can be taken as the value of a generated
random variable whose distribution is exactly that of the stationary distribution of
the Markov chain.

722    11   Simulation


Example 11.25 Consider a Markov chain with states 1, 2, 3 and suppose that
simulation yielded the values

                                    ‚éß
                                    ‚é®3,         if i = 1
                           N‚àí1 (i) = 2,         if i = 2
                                    ‚é©
                                     2,         if i = 3


and

                                    ‚éß
                                    ‚é®1,         if i = 1
                           N‚àí2 (i) = 3,         if i = 2
                                    ‚é©
                                     1,         if i = 3


Then

                                     ‚éß
                                     ‚é®3,        if i = 1
                            S‚àí2 (i) = 2,        if i = 2
                                     ‚é©
                                       3,       if i = 3


If

                                    ‚éß
                                    ‚é®3,         if i = 1
                           N‚àí3 (i) = 1,         if i = 2
                                    ‚é©
                                     1,         if i = 3


then

                                     ‚éß
                                     ‚é®3,        if i = 1
                            S‚àí3 (i) = 3,        if i = 2
                                     ‚é©
                                       3,       if i = 3


Therefore, no matter what the state is at time ‚àí3, the state at time 0 will be 3.   

Remark The procedure developed in this section for generating a random vari-
able whose distribution is the stationary distribution of the Markov chain is called
coupling from the past.

Explain why W1 , . . . , Wn has the same joint distribution as the order statistics of

19. Suppose we want to simulate a large number n of independent exponen-
tials with rate 1‚Äîcall them X1 , X2 , . . . , Xn . If we were to employ the inverse
transform technique we would require one logarithmic computation for each ex-
ponential generated. One way to avoid this is to first simulate Sn , a gamma
random variable with parameters (n, 1) (say, by the method of Section 11.3.3).
Now interpret Sn as the time of the nth event of a Poisson process with rate
1 and use the result that given Sn the set of the first n ‚àí 1 event times is
distributed as the set of n ‚àí 1 independent uniform (0, Sn ) random variables.
Based on this, explain why the following algorithm simulates n independent
exponentials:

  Step 1:    Generate Sn , a gamma random variable with parameters (n, 1).
  Step 2:    Generate n ‚àí 1 random numbers U1 , U2 , . . . , Un‚àí1 .
  Step 3:    Order the Ui , i = 1, . . . , n ‚àí 1 to obtain U(1) < U(2) < ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ < U(n‚àí1) .
  Step 4:    Let U(0) = 0, U(n) = 1, and set Xi = Sn (U(i) ‚àí U(i‚àí1) ),
             i = 1, . . . , n.

When the ordering (step 3) is performed according to the algorithm described in
Section 11.5, the preceding is an efficient method for simulating n exponentials
when all n are simultaneously required. If memory space is limited, however, and
the exponentials can be employed sequentially, discarding each exponential from
memory once it has been used, then the preceding may not be appropriate.
20. Consider the following procedure for randomly choosing a subset of size k
from the numbers 1, 2, . . . , n: Fix p and generate the first n time units of a renewal
process whose interarrival distribution is geometric with mean 1/p‚Äîthat is,
728     11   Simulation


P {interarrival time = k} = p(1 ‚àí p)k‚àí1 , k = 1, 2, . . . . Suppose events occur at
times i1 < i2 < ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ < im ‚©Ω n. If m = k, stop; i1 , . . . , im is the desired set.
If m > k, then randomly choose (by some method) a subset of size k from
i1 , . . . , im and then stop. If m < k, take i1 , . . . , im as part of the subset of size
k and then select (by some method) a random subset of size k ‚àí m from the set
{1, 2, . . . , n} ‚àí (i1 , . . . , im }. Explain why this algorithm works. As E[N (n)] = np
a reasonable choice of p is to take p ‚âà k/n. (This approach is due to Dieter.)
21. Consider the following algorithm for generating a random permutation of
the elements 1, 2, . . . , n. In this algorithm, P (i) can be interpreted as the element
in position i

  Step 1:     Set k = 1.
  Step 2:     Set P (1) = 1.
  Step 3:     If k = n, stop. Otherwise, let k = k + 1.
  Step 4:     Generate a random number U , and let

                                          P (k) = P ([kU ] + 1),
                              P ([kU ] + 1) = k.
                                        Go to step 3


  (a) Explain in words what the algorithm is doing.
  (b) Show that at iteration k‚Äîthat is, when the value of P (k) is initially set‚Äî
  that P (1), P (2), . . . , P (k) is a random permutation of 1, 2, . . . , k.
   Hint: Use induction and argue that

                      Pk {i1 , i2 , . . . , ij ‚àí1 , k, ij , . . . , ik‚àí2 , i}
                                                                                     1
                          = Pk‚àí1 {i1 , i2 , . . . , ij ‚àí1 , i, ij , . . . , ik‚àí2 }
                                                                                     k
                              1
                          =              by the induction hypothesis
                              k!
   The preceding algorithm can be used even if n is not initially known.

31. Consider a queueing system in which each service time, independent of the
past, has mean Œº. Let Wn and Dn denote, respectively, the amounts of time cus-
tomer n spends in the system and in queue. Hence, Dn = Wn ‚àí Sn where Sn is
the service time of customer n. Therefore,

                               E[Dn ] = E[Wn ] ‚àí Œº
                                                                   References     731

If we use simulation to estimate E[Dn ], should we
  (a) use the simulated data to determine Dn , which is then used as an estimate
  of E[Dn ]; or
  (b) use the simulated data to determine Wn and then use this quantity minus Œº
  as an estimate of E[Dn ]?
Repeat if we want to estimate E[Wn ].
*32. Show that if X and Y have the same distribution then

                            Var((X + Y )/2) ‚©Ω Var(X)

Hence, conclude that the use of antithetic variables can never increase variance
(though it need not be as efficient as generating an independent set of random
numbers).

after time t0 . Give an efficient simulation estimator of the expected additional
time after t0 until the system becomes empty.

64. See Section 5.2.3 of Chapter 5. Another way is to use moment generating
functions. The moment generating function of the sum of n independent expo-
nentials with rate Œª is equal to the product of their moment generating functions.
That is, it is [Œª/(Œª ‚àí t)]n . But this is precisely the moment generating function of
a gamma with parameters n and Œª.

23. Let X denote the first time a head appears. Let us obtain an equation for
E[N | X] by conditioning on the next two flips after X. This gives
      E[N | X] = E[N | X, h, h]p 2 + E[N | X, h, t]pq + E[N | X, t, h]pq
                  + E[N | X, t, t]q 2
where q = 1 ‚àí p. Now
          E[N | X, h, h] = X + 1,               E[N | X, h, t] = X + 1
          E[N | X, t, h] = X + 2,               E[N | X, t, t] = X + 2 + E[N ]
Substituting back gives
       E[N |X] = (X + 1)(p 2 + pq) + (X + 2) pq + (X + 2 + E[N ]) q 2
Taking expectations, and using the fact that X is geometric with mean 1/p, we
obtain
              E[N ] = 1 + p + q + 2pq + q 2 /p + 2q 2 + q 2 E[N ]

73. Condition on the value of the sum prior to going over 100. In all cases the
most likely value is 101. (For instance, if this sum is 98 then the final sum is
equally likely to be either 101, 102, 103, or 104. If the sum prior to going over is
95, then the final sum is 101 with certainty.)

Hence Pij = 0.

62.   (a) Since œÄi = 15 is equal to the inverse of the expected number of tran-
      sitions to return to state i, it follows that the expected number of steps to
      return to the original position is 5.
      (b) Condition on the first transition. Suppose it is to the right. In this case
      the probability is just the probability that a gambler who always bets 1
      and wins each bet with probability p will, when starting with 1, reach
      4 before going broke. By the gambler‚Äôs ruin problem this probability is
      equal to

                                        1 ‚àí q/p
                                       1 ‚àí (q/p)4

      Similarly, if the first move is to the left then the problem is again the same
      gambler‚Äôs ruin problem but with p and q reversed. The desired probability
      is thus
                             p‚àíq              q ‚àíp
                                       +
                          1 ‚àí (q/p)  4    1 ‚àí (p/q)4
                                   

84. There is a record whose value is between t and t + dt if the first X larger
than t lies between t and t + dt. From this we see that, independent of all record
values less than t, there will be one between t and t + dt with probability Œª(t) dt
where Œª(t) is the failure rate function given by
                                             f (t)
                                  Œª(t) =
                                           1 ‚àí F (t)
Since the counting process of record values has, by the preceding, independent
increments we can conclude (since there cannot be multiple record values because
the Xi are continuous) that it is a nonhomogeneous Poisson process with intensity
function Œª(t). When f is the exponential density, Œª(t) = Œª and so the counting
process of record values becomes an ordinary Poisson process with rate Œª.

19. There are four states. Let state 0 mean that no machines are down, state
1 that machine 1 is down and 2 is up, state 2 that machine 1 is up and 2
is down, and 3 that both machines are down. The balance equations are as
follows:

                             (Œª1 + Œª2 )P0 = Œº1 P1 + Œº2 P2
                             (Œº1 + Œª2 )P1 = Œª1 P0
                             (Œª1 + Œº2 )P2 = Œª2 P0 + Œº1 P3
                                    Œº1 P3 = Œª2 P1 + Œª1 P2
                       P0 + P1 + P2 + P3 = 1

The equations are easily solved and the proportion of time machine 2 is down is
P2 + P3 .

49. Think of each interarrival time as consisting of n independent phases‚Äîeach
of which is exponentially distributed with rate Œª‚Äîand consider the semi-Markov
process whose state at any time is the phase of the present interarrival time. Hence,
this semi-Markov process goes from state 1 to 2 to 3 . . . to n to 1, and so on. Also
the time spent in each state has the same distribution. Thus, clearly the limiting
probability of this semi-Markov chain is Pi = 1/n, i = 1, . . . , n. To compute
lim P {Y (t) < x}, we condition on the phase at time t and note that if it is n ‚àí i + 1,
which will be the case with probability 1/n, then the time until a renewal occurs
will be sum of i exponential phases, which will thus have a gamma distribution
with parameters i and Œª.

time of a service. If a customer leaves the system empty, the time until the next
departure is the time until an arrival plus the time of a service.

36. The distributions of the queue size and busy period are the same for all three
disciplines; that of the waiting time is different. However, the means are identical.
This can be seen by using W = L/Œª, since L is the same for all. The smallest
variance in the waiting time occurs under first-come, first-served and the largest
under last-come, first-served.

45. By regarding any breakdowns that occur during a service as being part of
that service, we see that this is an M/G/1 model. We need to calculate the first two
moments of a service time. Now the time of a service is the time T until something
happens (either a service completion or a breakdown) plus any additional time A.
Thus,

                               E[S] = E[T + A]
                                    = E[T ] + E[A]

To compute E[A], we condition upon whether the happening is a service or a
breakdown. This gives

                                   Œº                      Œ±
            E[A] = E[A | service]     + E[A | breakdown]
                                 Œº+Œ±                     Œº+Œ±
                                      Œ±
                  = E[A | breakdown]
                                     Œº+Œ±
                              
                      1            Œ±
                  =     + E[S]
                      Œ≤          Œº+Œ±

                                1      1          Œ±
                      E[S] =       +     + E[S]
                               Œ±+Œº     Œ≤         Œº+Œ±

or

                                          1   Œ±
                                 E[S] =     +
                                          Œº ŒºŒ≤

                     E[S 2 ] = E[(T + A)2 ]
                            = E[T 2 ] + 2E[AT ] + E[A2 ]
                            = E[T 2 ] + 2E[A]E[T ] + E[A2 ]

The independence of A and T follows because the time of the first happening is
independent of whether the happening was a service or a breakdown. Now,

In the preceding, S ‚àó is the additional service needed after the breakdown is over
and S ‚àó has the same distribution as S. The preceding also uses the fact that the
expected square of an exponential is twice the square of its mean.
      Another way of calculating the moments of S is to use the representation

                                
                                N
                           S=     (Ti + Bi ) + TN +1
                                i=1

where N is the number of breakdowns while a customer is in service, Ti is the
time starting when service commences for the ith time until a happening occurs,
and Bi is the length of the ith breakdown. We now use the fact that, given N , all
of the random variables in the representation are independent exponentials with
the Ti having rate Œº + Œ± and the Bi having rate Œ≤. This yields
                                        N +1 N

Let N denote the number of minimal path sets having all of its components func-

both cases we can compute expressions for E[N ] and E[N 2 ] by writing N as the
sum of indicator (i.e., Bernoulli) random variables. Then we can use the inequality
to derive bounds on r(p).
